title: Quantum Science Signals
summary: Quantum science is the signal layer for measurement, computation, sensing, and the strange physical rules beneath advanced technology.

items.name: Error correction is the central technical challenge
items.summary: Quantum computing progress is now mostly about making fragile hardware useful through better coding and control.
items.whyItMatters: The road to utility runs through error correction, not just qubit counts.

items.name: Quantum sensing is often closer to practical value
items.summary: Precision measurement applications may mature faster than universal quantum computation.
items.whyItMatters: The first useful quantum products may be sensors rather than computers.

items.name: Multiple hardware modalities are competing
items.summary: Superconducting, trapped-ion, neutral-atom, and photonic approaches all have tradeoffs.
items.whyItMatters: The field still has not settled on the dominant implementation path.

items.name: IBM
items.summary: A major platform for quantum hardware, software, and research access.
items.whyItMatters: IBM keeps quantum research connected to enterprise-grade tooling and ecosystem access.

items.name: Google Quantum AI
items.summary: Central to superconducting qubit research and quantum error correction.
items.whyItMatters: It remains one of the main reference points for the hardware frontier.

items.name: PsiQuantum
items.summary: Represents the photonic approach to scalable quantum computing.
items.whyItMatters: It shows how different physical architectures are still competing for dominance.

items.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
items.summary: A major standards and measurement anchor for quantum research.
items.whyItMatters: Quantum progress depends on measurement precision and standards discipline.

items.name: CSIRO
items.summary: Australia has a meaningful quantum research and commercialisation footprint.
items.whyItMatters: It is one of the key nodes linking local research to commercial capability.

items.name: DARPA
items.summary: A long-running funder of high-risk quantum and advanced technology programs.
items.whyItMatters: Quantum has remained strategically interesting enough to justify defense funding.

items.name: John Preskill
items.summary: One of the clearest explainers of quantum information and error correction.
items.whyItMatters: He is a key interpreter of what quantum computing might actually become.

items.name: Michelle Simmons
items.summary: A major figure in Australia's quantum ecosystem and silicon quantum research.
items.whyItMatters: She is a major signal for the Australian quantum stack.

items.name: David Deutsch
items.summary: A foundational thinker in quantum computation.
items.whyItMatters: His ideas helped make quantum computing a serious field of study.

items.name: When will quantum computing become useful?
items.summary: The field still debates whether utility will arrive first in chemistry, optimisation, sensing, or something else.
items.whyItMatters: Utility, not novelty, will determine the real impact of the field.

items.name: How many hardware paths will survive?
items.summary: Not every modality will win; the question is which physical implementation scales best.
items.whyItMatters: Architecture choices determine the shape of the market and research agenda.

items.name: What is the right benchmark for progress?
items.summary: People argue over supremacy, utility, error-corrected logical qubits, and application-level value.
items.whyItMatters: Benchmarks can either clarify progress or distort it.
